elements.sent-2411-001: Davide Basso 2\textsuperscript{nd Luca Bortolussi \IEEEauthorblockA{University of Trieste Trieste, Italy davide.basso@phd.units.it, lbortolussi@units.it \and \IEEEauthorblockN{3\textsuperscript{rd Mirjana Videnovic-Misic \IEEEauthorblockA{Infineon Technologies AT Villach, Austria mirjana.videnovic-misic@infineon.com \and \IEEEauthorblockN{4\textsuperscript{th Husni Habal \IEEEauthorblockA{Infineon Technologies AG Munich, Germany husni.habal@infineon.com \makeatletter \newcommand{\linebreakand { \end{@IEEEauthorhalign \par \begin{@IEEEauthorhalign \makeatother \bstctlcite{IEEEexample:BSTcontrol.}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-002: Analog integrated circuit (IC) floorplanning is typically a manual process with the placement of components (devices and modules) planned by a layout engineer.
elements.sent-2411-003: This process is further complicated by the interdependence of floorplanning and routing steps, numerous electric and layout-dependent constraints, as well as the high level of customization expected in analog design.
elements.sent-2411-004: This paper presents a novel automatic floorplanning algorithm based on reinforcement learning.
elements.sent-2411-005: It is augmented by a relational graph convolutional neural network model for encoding circuit features and positional constraints.
elements.sent-2411-006: The combination of these two machine learning methods enables knowledge transfer across different circuit designs with distinct topologies and constraints, increasing the generalization ability of the solution.
elements.sent-2411-007: Applied to $6$ industrial circuits, our approach surpassed established floorplanning techniques in terms of speed, area and half-perimeter wire length.
elements.sent-2411-008: When integrated into a procedural generator for layout completion, overall layout time was reduced by $67.3\%$ with a $8.3\%$ mean area reduction compared to manual layout.
elements.sent-2411-009: Designing the layout of analog circuits is a crucial and complex task requiring significant expertise due to their susceptibility to noise, parasitics, alongside stringent topological requirements.
elements.sent-2411-010: This often leads to multiple iterations for layout engineers to achieve an optimal result.
elements.sent-2411-011: The procedure involves two distinct yet closely entangled steps to place and connect circuit devices, called floorplanning and routing respectively.
elements.sent-2411-012: Metaheuristics as simulated annealing (SA), particle swarm optimization (PSO), and genetic algorithms (GA) \cite{singh_review_2016 have been employed to streamline the floorplanning step.}
elements.sent-2411-013: However, a primary drawback is that they cannot utilize past or external knowledge to enhance exploration of the solution space, as they optimize each problem instance anew.
elements.sent-2411-014: Works as \cite{li_customized_2020 instead opted to leverage machine learning (ML) based solutions, specifically Graph Neural Networks (GNNs), to improve the generalization capabilities in generating optimal floorplans spanning across diverse circuit topologies.}
elements.sent-2411-015: Reinforcement learning (RL) techniques have become effective in tackling combinatorial problems \cite{RL4CO , such as floorplanning, by optimally navigating and focusing on the most promising solution space regions.}
elements.sent-2411-016: Since floorplanning can be framed as a sequential decision-making process within a Markov Decision Process (MDP), RL techniques have led to state-of-the-art outcomes in several digital layout applications \cite{lai_maskplace_2022,cheng_joint_2021,amini_generalizable_2022,lai_chipformer_2023,yang_miracle_2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-017: Nevertheless, the use of RL in analog layout remains limited, calling for further exploration.
elements.sent-2411-018: In this paper, we propose a combination of Relational Graph Convolutional Neural Networks (R-GCNs) \cite{schlichtkrull_modeling_2017 and RL to create optimal floorplans for diverse analog circuits types and topologies.}
elements.sent-2411-019: The R-GCN model provides detailed circuit data information to the RL agent, which combines them with spatial encodings from a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) \cite{oshea2015introduction to determine the best shape and placement for each component.}
elements.sent-2411-020: We also integrate this methodology with the ANAGEN procedural generator framework \cite{passerini_anagen_2021, demiri_anagen_2023 and an Obstacle Avoiding Rectilinear Steiner Tree (OARSMT) global router, as in \cite{basso_layout_2024 , streamlining the pipeline for automatic analog IC layout generation.}}
elements.sent-2411-021: The workflow overview is depicted in Figure \ref{fig:auto_layout_overview .}
elements.sent-2411-022: The key contributions of this paper are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-023: We train an R-GCN model to predict rewards for circuit placements.
elements.sent-2411-024: Once trained, it serves as an encoder of circuit, device, and geometric constraints for RL agent use to guarantee optimal generalization capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-025: We present an RL agent that combines graph and pixel-level representations to comprehensively describe circuit and problem characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-026: The agent policy is designed to learn to select optimal shapes and positions for components while ensuring no overlaps and adherence to constraints such as symmetry and alignment.
elements.sent-2411-027: To our knowledge, this is the first time such approach is proposed.
elements.sent-2411-028: We validate our novel method on circuits of increasing complexity.
elements.sent-2411-029: Our approach outperforms traditional metaheuristics and existing RL-based methods in terms of area, proxy wirelength, speed and cost metrics.
elements.sent-2411-030: Our enhanced procedural layout generation pipeline consistently shortens design timeframes while matching and even surpassing the quality of manually designed layouts.
elements.sent-2411-031: The remainder of this paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-033: Background of GNNs and RL are introduced in Section \ref{sec:Background .}
elements.sent-2411-034: Section \ref{sec:Methodology details the problem setting and our R-GCN and RL-based floorplanning approach.}
elements.sent-2411-035: Experimental results are provided in Section \ref{sec:Results , while conclusions are drawn in Section \ref{sec:Conclusions .}}
elements.sent-2411-036: Floorplanning automation has extensively relied on metaheuristic techniques such as SA, GA or PSO that, combined with topological model like Sequence Pair (SP) \cite{balasa_symmetry_2000 or B*-Tree \cite{shunmugathammal_novel_2020 to describe component's relative placement, targeted to minimize an objective function through a stochastic search process.}}
elements.sent-2411-037: Although producing compact floorplans, these methods leave insufficient space for routing tracks, ultimately resulting in unusable layouts.
elements.sent-2411-038: Additionally, implementing geometric constraints is challenging and tends to increase the already lengthy optimization runtimes, especially with more complex circuits.
elements.sent-2411-039: Recently, learning-based methodologies have emerged as promising alternatives.
elements.sent-2411-040: For instance, \cite{li_customized_2020 trained a GNN to predict analog ICs performance based on device placement, plugging it into an SA optimizer, but did not use it to directly produce a floorplan or consider positional constraints.}
elements.sent-2411-041: Gusm\~{a o et al. in \cite{de_gusmao_scalable_2022 developed an unsupervised encoder-decoder model using attention mechanisms and R-GCNs to embed topological constraints (symmetry and proximity) and generate floorplans; yet, validation to remove overlaps is required and no routing related optimization metric is considered.}}
elements.sent-2411-042: Ahmadi et al. \cite{ahmadi_analog_2021 trained an RL agent to place FinFET modules on a grid, minimizing symmetry and alignment errors, area, and wirelength, but the assumption of fixed device shapes limited the approach's flexibility.}
elements.sent-2411-043: This work addresses the aforementioned limitations.
elements.sent-2411-044: Circuit netlists and layouts can be represented as graphs, leading EDA methods to benefit from applying GNNs to various design stages \cite{servadei_GNN_2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-045: A graph is described as a tuple $G = (V,E)$, where $V$ is the set of nodes and $E$ is the set of edges.
elements.sent-2411-046: The neighborhood of node $v$, denoted as $\mathcal{N (v)$, is $ u | (u,v) \in E$.}
elements.sent-2411-047: A graph is directed if edge direction matters; otherwise, it is undirected.
elements.sent-2411-048: A graph with multiple types of nodes or edges is defined as heterogeneous, whereas one with a single type is homogeneous.
elements.sent-2411-049: GNNs are deep learning models that natively operate on graph-structured inputs, aiming to learn continuous embedding vectors per node through a message passing process \cite{gilmer2017neural .}
elements.sent-2411-050: Nodes exchange vector-based messages with adjacent nodes over iterations, progressively enriching their state with neighborhood context.
elements.sent-2411-051: Graph Convolutional Neural Networks (GCNs) \cite{kipf_semi-supervised_2016 aggregate features as follows: \begin{equation}h_u^{(l+1)} = \sigma \left(\sum_{v\in \mathcal{N}(u)}\frac{h_v^{(l)} W^{(l)}}{c_u}\right),\end{equation} being $h_u^{(l+1)}$ the updated node feature vector, $\sigma$ a non-linear differentiable function, $l$ the l-th GCN layer, $h_v^{(l)}$ the neighboring nodes feature vector, $W^{(l) \in \mathbb{R ^{d \times d}$ a learnable weight matrix, and $c_u$ a normalization factor.}}}
elements.sent-2411-052: R-GCN extend the aggregation process to heterogeneous graphs, accounting for different node relationships: \begin{equation}h_u^{(l+1)} = \sigma \left(W_0^{(l)}\cdot h_u^{(l)} + \sum_{r \in R} \sum_{v\in \mathcal{N}^r(u)} \frac{W_r^{(l)}\cdot h_v^{(l)}}{c_{u,r}}\right),\end{equation} where $\mathcal{N ^r(u)$ is the set of neighbors of node $u$ under relation $r \in R$, $W_0^{(l) \in \mathbb{R}$ is a learnable weight vector for a node’s self-connection in each layer, and finally $c_{u,r}$ is again a normalization constant that varies with the R-GCN task.}}
elements.sent-2411-053: RL techniques train an agent to discover effective solutions through interactions with the environment, utilizing an MDP framework denoted as $(\mathcal{S , \mathcal{A , \mathcal{P , \mathcal{R , \gamma)$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-054: At each time step $t$, the RL agent is in a state $s_t \in \mathcal{S}$ and chooses an action $a_t \in \mathcal{A}$ to execute.
elements.sent-2411-055: Following the transition probability $\mathcal{P}$, the agent transitions to a new state $s_{t+1}$ and receives a reward $r_t \in \mathcal{R}$ indicating the impact of its action, discounted by $\gamma$, to balance the relevance of immediate versus future rewards.
elements.sent-2411-056: The agent eventually learns an optimal policy $\pi^*(a|s)$ that maximizes the expected sum of discounted rewards $G_t = \sum\limits_{k=0 ^\infty \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$.}
elements.sent-2411-057: Enhancing the floorplanning algorithm by Basso et al. in \cite{basso_layout_2024 , we propose an integration of an R-GCN model for chip representation learning with an RL agent.}
elements.sent-2411-058: The agent selects a shape of a circuit components and places it on a discretized grid corresponding to the layout space.
elements.sent-2411-059: The floorplanning problem is formulated as the following MDP:.
elements.sent-2411-060: States: The state $s_t$ combines detailed information encoded by the R-GCN model, such as circuit graph $g$ and current instance $n_k$ $32$-dimensional embeddings, as well as local feature maps extracted at the pixel level by a CNN.
elements.sent-2411-061: The local feature maps consist of a $32{\times 32$ grid representation $f_g \in \{0,1\}^{32 \times 32}$, and two reward-related masks showing the increase in the placement empty space and wirelength proxy metric, similar to \cite{lai_maskplace_2022 , respectively denoted as $f_{ds , f_w \in [0,1]^{32 \times 32}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-062: Additionally, there are three positional masks $f_{p \in \{0, 1\}^{3\times32\times32}$, used also for action masking, delineating the admissible placement cells for the next block given the three possible shapes and adherence to non-overlapping and optionally defined spatial constraints.}
elements.sent-2411-063: Actions: The action $a_t$ at time step $t$ consists in selecting one of three possible shapes for the current block $b_t$ and determining the grid cell to place its lower left corner.
elements.sent-2411-064: Rewards: We define a partial reward $r_t$ as the negative increase of proxy wirelength and empty space in the floorplan after $a_t$.
elements.sent-2411-065: The end of episode reward is instead equal to the negative weighted sum of floorplan's area, half-perimeter wirelength and, if specified, discrepancy w.r.t.
elements.subsection-2411-004: Preliminary Structure Recognition (SR) and Functional Block Configuration
elements.sent-2411-066: Given an input schematic, we use Infineon's GCN-based SR tool \cite{patel_machine_nodate to detect circuit functional blocks.}
elements.sent-2411-067: Following \cite{basso_layout_2024 , we generate different block shapes by keeping a fixed total device width, i.e. area, and tailoring internal routing and device placement based on the recognized functional structure.}
elements.sent-2411-068: These configurations are then provided to the RL agent.
elements.sent-2411-069: An optimal floorplanning algorithm should be capable to generalize its performances across various circuit configurations and constraints.
elements.sent-2411-070: R-GCNs can effectively handle graphs of varying dimensions and topology, thanks to their permutation invariance.
elements.sent-2411-071: Works as \cite{amini_generalizable_2022, mirhoseini_graph_2021, le_toward_2023 proposed to pre-train a GNN model on the supervised task of predicting rewards of input circuit graphs.}
elements.sent-2411-072: By aligning training tasks with the RL agent's goals, circuit embeddings produced for subsequent stages capture meaningful signals, enhancing RL agent's decision-making with augmented generalization capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-073: In our setting, shown in Figure \ref{fig:circuit_graph , circuits are represented as undirected graphs where each node $v_i$ corresponds to a functional block or single device.}
elements.sent-2411-074: The edges $e_i$ represent relationships between nodes $(u,v)$, which can be connectivity (if they are connected in the netlist), horizontal or vertical alignment, or horizontal or vertical symmetry.
elements.sent-2411-075: A node feature vector $x_u \in X$ includes the block area, internal parameters like transistor or resistance stripe width, terminals routing direction, pin counts, and a 28-dimensional one-hot encoding of the block's functional structure (e.g., current mirror, differential pair, cascode, etc.).
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=.9\columnwidth]{paper_body/images/circuit_graph.pdf} \caption{8-structure OTA circuit schematic with its graph representation. Violet edges are for vertical alignment and black for connectivity. Nodes are colored according to the functional block type.} \label{fig:circuit_graph}
elements.sent-2411-076: Figure \ref{fig:rgcn_arch shows the R-GCN model architecture, which consists of $4$ R-GCN layers followed by a node mean aggregation block to produce the whole graph embedding.}
elements.sent-2411-077: This is then fed into $5$ fully connected (FC) layers to predict the reward value.
elements.sent-2411-078: The R-GCN training dataset comprises $21600$ floorplans and corresponding reward values, generated by optimizing placement w.r.t.
elements.sent-2411-079: The circuits of interest vary in size and complexity, including operational transconductance amplifiers (OTAs), bias circuits, drivers, level shifters, clock synchronizers, comparators, and oscillators.
elements.sent-2411-080: Moreover, we ensured a balance between constrained and unconstrained floorplans.
elements.sent-2411-081: The supervised model is trained to minimize the mean squared error between the ground truth and predicted reward associated with the input circuit graph.
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width = .935\textwidth]{paper_body/images/GNNRL_arch.pdf} \caption{Overview of the RL model, enriched with CNN based feature extractor and policy network.} \label{fig:rgcn_RL_arch}
elements.sent-2411-082: Given the R-GCN trained model, we remove the final FC layers and use the remaining part as encoder for the RL agent, enhancing the transfer learning capabilities of our methodology.
elements.sent-2411-083: Figure \ref{fig:rgcn_RL_arch provides an overview of the RL architecture.}
elements.sent-2411-084: We train the RL agent using a masked version of Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) \cite{schulman_proximal_2017 , a state-of-the-art on-policy algorithm.}
elements.sent-2411-085: Large action spaces can hinder RL convergence towards an optimal policy.
elements.sent-2411-086: Therefore, we discretize the layout canvas into a $32{\times 32$ grid $f_g$, effectively balancing performance and accuracy by containing the action space while ensuring constraints satisfaction.}
elements.sent-2411-087: The grid height $H$ and width $W$ are computed as $W{= H{=}\sqrt{\frac{\sum_{i=1 ^m A_i {R_{\text{max }$ being $A_i$ the area of the $i^{th}$ circuit device and $R_{\text{max {= 11$ the maximum empirically derived aspect ratio for a floorplan.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-088: Since both $H$ and $W$ depend on the size of each functional block, this design choice accommodates any complex circuit placement.
elements.sent-2411-089: The agent can choose from $3$ candidate shapes for a circuit structure, similar to the flexibility human designers have.
elements.sent-2411-090: Combined with selecting the cell for placing the lower-left corner of a block, this results in an action space $\mathcal{A}$ of size $3{\times 32{\times 32{= 3072$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-091: To prevent further escalation of action space dimensionality, we use a heuristic inspired by \cite{mirhoseini_graph_2021 , which arranges block placement in order of decreasing size.\looseness=-1 As mentioned earlier, our floorplanning methodology can handle fundamental positional requirements such as symmetry, alignment, and guarantee the absence of device overlap.}
elements.sent-2411-092: Works as \cite{huang_invalid_2022 prove that policy gradient algorithms can take advantage of masking procedures to avoid selecting invalid actions based on state information.}
elements.sent-2411-093: Therefore, at each episode step, we generate three positional masks $f_{p}$, one for each possible candidate shape.
elements.sent-2411-094: These masks are obtained by combining two binary matrices: one representing partial placement and the other symmetry or alignment constraint masks.
elements.sent-2411-095: In the first one, a value of $1$ indicates an available cell, while $0$ signifies an already occupied one.
elements.sent-2411-096: The second one designates with $1\text{s}$ where the corresponding constraint would be satisfied (based on the placement of blocks belonging to a constraint group and the corresponding symmetry or alignment axis) and $0\text{s}$ where it would not.
elements.sent-2411-097: We map the real sizes of each circuit instance $(w, h)$ without approximation, where $w_g$ and $h_g$ are the respective scaled width and height on the grid: \begin{equation}w_g=\ceil[\Bigg]{\frac{w\times32}{W}}, h_g= \ceil[\Bigg]{\frac{h\times32}{H}}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-098: Given that the R-GCN model does not supply detailed location information for specific circuit instances during an episode, we enrich the agent's state representation.
elements.sent-2411-099: As mentioned in Section \ref{subsec:problem_formulation , this is achieved by combining the current node, i.e. block $b_t$, and graph, i.e. circuit, embeddings $n_k$ and $g$ with $6$ additional grid-based masks: $f_g$, $f_w$, $f_{ds}$ and $f_p$, which reflect partial placements, wire and dead space, i.e. empty space, increases, and valid positions.}
elements.sent-2411-100: This augmented state aids the RL agent in identifying the optimal placement to optimize rewards.
elements.sent-2411-101: Lai et al. \cite{lai_maskplace_2022 first proposed this approach, using $f_p$, $f_g$ and computing $f_w$ as the increase in proxy wirelength when placing $b_t$ in a specific position.}
elements.sent-2411-102: Our approach extends the positional mask $f_p$ to account for multiple device shapes and introduces the dead space mask $f_{ds}$, a normalized, continuous matrix indicating the increase in empty space if $b_t$ is placed in a certain location.
elements.sent-2411-103: To construct $f_{ds}$, we iterate over all available cells on the placement grid, compute the resulting dead space from placing $b_t$, and subtract the previous dead space value.
elements.sent-2411-104: Already occupied locations are set to the maximum increment $1$ to mask invalid positions.
elements.sent-2411-105: Figure \ref{fig:wire_dspace_masks provides a visual example of $f_w$ and $f_{ds}$.}
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width = .85\columnwidth]{paper_body/images/masks.pdf} \caption{Dead space (left) and wire (right) masks. Darker areas highlight higher rewards regions in the case a block is placed there.} \label{fig:wire_dspace_masks}
elements.sent-2411-106: Treating grid masks as analogous to images, as suggested by \cite{cheng_joint_2021 , leverages CNNs' effectiveness in generating informative embeddings.}
elements.sent-2411-107: For this reason, we concatenate the masks forming a single tensor of dimension $6{\times 32{\times 32$ and feed them to a CNN.}}
elements.sent-2411-108: The convolution layers use a $3{\times 3$ kernel size with stride of $1$, padding of $1$ and $16,32,32,64,64$ filter channels, followed by one FC layer to produce a $512$-dimensional embedding vector.}
elements.sent-2411-109: Finally, after concatenating the R-GCN embedding and CNN outputs, this compact state representation is fed into both value and policy network.
elements.sent-2411-110: The policy includes a single FC layer that converts the input vector to a $512$-dimensional one, $3$ deconvolution layers with a kernel size of $4\times4$, a stride of $2$, a padding of $1$ and $32$, $16$, and $8$ filter channels.
elements.sent-2411-111: The policy then generates a probability distribution over actions, allowing the agent to jointly decide the shape and location of the new device to be placed.
elements.sent-2411-112: As delineated in Section \ref{subsec:problem_formulation , the agent's goal is to minimize two primary metrics: area occupation and half-perimeter wirelength (HPWL).}
elements.sent-2411-113: HPWL, a widely used approximation for true wirelength, is computed as the half-perimeter of all nets' bounding boxes: \begin{equation}\label{eq:HPWL} \text{HPWL} = \sum_{i=1}^n \text{max}(x_i)-\text{min}(x_i) + \text{max}(y_i)-\text{min}(y_i),\end{equation} where $x_i$, $y_i$ are the endpoints of a net, and $n$ is the total count of nets in the netlist.
elements.sent-2411-114: The dead space DS in a floorplan $F$ is computed as $1 - \frac{\sum_{i=1 ^m A_i {F_{area }$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-115: To better guide the agent during an episode rollout, we provide intermediate rewards $r_t$ based on the increase in partial floorplan dead space and HPWL, computed from the currently placed instances after possibly taking action $a_t$.
elements.sent-2411-116: The intermediate reward is defined as: \begin{equation}\label{eq:partial_reward} r_t = -(\Delta_{ds} + \Delta_{\text{HPWL}}),\end{equation} where $\Delta_{ds =\text{DS _t - \text{DS _{t-1}$ and $\Delta_{\text{HPWL =\text{HPWL _t - \text{HPWL _{t-1}$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-117: Given the optional constraint of a fixed aspect ratio for the final floorplan, we define the agent's end of episode reward $\mathcal{R}$ as: \begin{equation}\label{eq:reward_func} \mathcal{R} = -\left( \alpha \frac{F_\text{area}}{\sum_{i=1}^m A_i} + \beta \frac{\text{HPWL}}{\text{HPWL\textsubscript{min}}} + \gamma(R^* - R)^2 \right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-118: Here, $\alpha$, $\beta$ and $\gamma$ are empirically set weights ($1$, $5$, and $5$, respectively) determined through extensive experimentation.
elements.sent-2411-119: These weights balance area, wirelength and fixed outline error terms in terms of their magnitude and impact on the final floorplan quality.
elements.sent-2411-120: Finally, whenever the generated floorplan violates any predefined constraint, we penalize the agent's behavior with a reward of $-50$.
elements.sent-2411-121: Our methodology is designed to develop a single RL agent capable of generating optimal floorplans for a wide array of circuit types and constraints.
elements.sent-2411-122: To achieve this, we use a hybrid curriculum learning (HCL) approach, described in \cite{zaremba_learning_2015 , which incrementally presents the agent circuits of growing complexity.}
elements.sent-2411-123: Starting with smaller circuits, we interleave them with random sampling of new circuit instances and constraints.
elements.sent-2411-124: This method maintains the agent's exposure to complex scenarios and prevents the loss of previously acquired knowledge, thereby enhancing transferability to new, unseen instances.
elements.sent-2411-125: The circuits used to train the RL are $3$ operational transconductance amplifiers (OTAs) and $2$ bias ones, respectively encompassing $3,5,8,3\text{ and 9$ blocks, to ensure enough diversity in the data.}
elements.sent-2411-126: Once a feasible floorplan is generated, we construct an OARSMT for each net to minimize wirelength and avoid obstacles.
elements.sent-2411-127: Unlike \cite{basso_layout_2024 , which required congestion estimation to reserve space for routing channels, our method yields routing ready floorplans.}
elements.sent-2411-128: The global routing tree is segmented into conduits, detailing connections and layers, guiding ANAGEN's router to finalize circuit connections.
elements.sent-2411-129: This approach enhances ANAGEN's capabilities, potentially making it competitive with current state-of-the-art techniques \cite{magical, dhar_align_2020 , while preserving the correctness inherent in procedural generation and leveraging RL for advanced floorplan generation.}
elements.sent-2411-130: Our floorplanning pipeline is built in Python 3.9, using DGL \cite{wang2019dgl and Stable Baselines3 \cite{stable-baselines3 libraries for implementing respectively the R-GCN and RL models.}}
elements.figure-2411-006: \includegraphics[width = .93\columnwidth]{paper_body/images/RL_training.pdf} \caption{Episode Reward Mean and Approximate Kullback-Leibler divergence during RL agent's HCL training schedule.} \label{fig:rollout_mean}
elements.sent-2411-131: To foster policy robustness and reduce convergence time, we use $16$ parallel environments to gather multiple experiences.
elements.sent-2411-132: Following the HCL approach from Section \ref{subsec:RL_training , we train each circuit for $4096$ episodes.}
elements.sent-2411-133: During the first half of the episodes we avoid introducing new constraints or circuits.
elements.sent-2411-134: After this phase, we begin sampling new circuit instances and constraints with probabilities $p_{\text{circuit =0.5 \text{ and p_{\text{constraint =0.3$, respectively.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-135: Figure \ref{fig:rollout_mean illustrates the benefits of the HCL procedure for convergence towards a stable policy.}
elements.sent-2411-136: The agent can recover optimal rewards and achieve low approximate Kullback-Leibler divergence values during circuit sampling, indicating strong generalization and robustness.
elements.sent-2411-137: Training the RL agent on a single Nvidia A30 GPU took 12 hours and 42 minutes.
elements.sent-2411-138: We evaluate our approach against established metaheuristics such as SA, the same methodology used by state-of-the-art automatic layout generator \cite{dhar_align_2020 , PSO, GA as well as the two methods from \cite{basso_layout_2024 , involving a combination of RL with SA and a pure RL technique both based on SP.}}
elements.sent-2411-139: Unfortunately, the circuits of interest are incompatible with other fully automated layout generation engines \cite{magical, dhar_align_2020 due to technology constraints.}
elements.sent-2411-140: Performances are measured on $3$ industrial designs from the RL training set and $3$ unseen ones, aiming to validate transferability with zero-shot and few-shot fine-tuning on novel circuits.
elements.sent-2411-141: Few-shot learning involves refining a pre-trained RL agent by continuing its training on a specific problem or circuit instance, optimizing it for that context.
elements.sent-2411-142: For a fair comparison, congestion-aware device spacing is applied to all other approaches to allocate sufficient room for routing channels, as our methodology provides routing-ready floorplans.
elements.sent-2411-143: No constraints are imposed on any circuit.
elements.sent-2411-144: Table \ref{tab:GNNRLvsOthers lists the interquartile mean and standard deviation values for algorithm computational runtime, floorplan HPWL, dead space, and associated reward.}
elements.sent-2411-145: We emphasize the best results in bold, and highlight the second and third best rewards in italic underlined and underlined only, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-146: Unseen circuits are represented as grey rows.
elements.sent-2411-147: As shown, our novel approach surpasses the baselines in terms of floorplan reward in all scenarios with proper fine-tuning, and in $4$ out of $6$ cases at zero-shot while significantly improving runtime.
elements.sent-2411-148: Moreover, our methodology demonstrates remarkable transfer capability across unseen and more complex designs, even without additional fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-149: On new circuits, HPWL and dead space percentage are reduced by $38.7\%$ and $66.8\%$, respectively, compared to past techniques.
elements.sent-2411-150: Moreover, few-shot fine-tuning improves results compared to the zero-shot model for the same number of iterations.
elements.sent-2411-151: While training time is significant for few-shot solutions, once fine-tuned, the model doesn't require retraining, making single floorplan generation runtimes comparable to zero-shot ones and outperforming other approaches.\looseness=-1.
elements.figure-2411-008: \subfloat[]{\includegraphics[width=0.34\columnwidth]{paper_body/images/driver_abstract_output.png}\label{fig:abstract_out}} \subfloat[]{\includegraphics[width=0.32\columnwidth]{paper_body/images/driver_layout_template_fromRL.png}\label{fig:template_RL}} \subfloat[]{\includegraphics[width=0.33\columnwidth]{paper_body/images/driver_layout_fromRL.png}\label{fig:layout_RL}} \subfloat[]{\includegraphics[width=0.49\columnwidth]{paper_body/images/driver_layout_optimized.png}\label{fig:RL_opt_driver}} \hspace*{0.006\columnwidth} \subfloat[]{\includegraphics[width=0.5\columnwidth]{paper_body/images/qgen_manual_driver.png}\label{fig:manual_driver}} \caption{(a) RL-generated placement and OARSMT global routing, (b) post-adjustment floorplan and channels definitions, (c) resulting layout, (d) post-manual refinement optimized layout, and (e) full manual design.}\label{fig:Driver}
elements.figure-2411-009: \caption{Comparison of Area, Dead Space, and Layout Generation Time Between Our Automated Method and Manual Design.} \label{tab:practical_examples} \columnwidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{@{}ccccccc@{}} Circuit Method Area ($\text{\textmu m}^2$) Dead space (\%) \begin{tabular}[c]{@{}c@{}}Template generation time (s)\end{tabular} \begin{tabular}[c]{@{}c@{}}Manual improvement time (h)\end{tabular} \begin{tabular}[c]{@{}c@{}}Final layout generation time (h)\end{tabular} \multirow{2}{*}{OTA} Ours 228.6 (-14.1\%) 30.01 (-5.98\%) 111.0 0.17 0.20 (-97.5\%) Manual 266.0 31.92 - - 8 \multirow{2}{*}{Bias-1} Ours 515.6 (+52.1\%) 54.01 (+8.68\%) 127.1 1 1.04 (-87.0\%) Manual 247.1 49.32 - - 8 \multirow{2}{*}{Driver} Ours 3584.7 (-2.43\%) 38.78 (-3.82\%) 456.3 20 20.13 (-37.1\%) Manual 3674.0 40.32 - - 32 \end{tabular} }
elements.sent-2411-152: We plug our novel floorplanning algorithm into the pipeline proposed in \cite{basso_layout_2024 and validate its effectiveness by comparing the completed layouts of a 3-block OTA, 9-block Bias, and 17-block Driver from \cite{demiri_anagen_2023 against their manually designed counterparts.}}
elements.sent-2411-153: Notably, the manual Bias layout was crafted without using ANAGEN, unlike the other two.
elements.sent-2411-154: The metrics of interest involve floorplan's area, dead space, and time required to produce a DRC and LVS clean layout.
elements.sent-2411-155: Results in Table \ref{tab:practical_examples underscore algorithm's capability to quickly generate valid floorplans, facilitating faster design iteration.}
elements.sent-2411-156: The OTA and Driver circuits exhibit improved area and dead space metrics, although the Bias circuit has higher dead space percentages.
elements.sent-2411-157: This latter layout in fact benefits from the absence of ANAGEN's routing constraints, resulting in smaller area occupation.
elements.sent-2411-158: Finally, Figure \ref{fig:Driver shows a direct comparison between the automatically generated Driver layout from our methodology and the manually designed one.}
elements.sent-2411-159: Figures \ref{fig:abstract_out and \ref{fig:template_RL illustrate the outcome of our floorplanning and global routing algorithm, providing clear guidance for physical design engineers on the expected wireflow.}}
elements.sent-2411-160: In more complex layouts, manual refinement of routing channels guided by the OARSMT is still necessary to accommodate ANAGEN's routing sensitivities.
elements.sent-2411-161: However, for simpler layouts like the OTA example, routing channel generation was fully automated.
elements.sent-2411-162: Improving this aspect remains a focus for future work.\looseness=-1.
elements.sent-2411-163: This paper proposes a combined R-GCN and RL-based methodology for analog ICs floorplanning, ensuring alignment, symmetry, and no overlap constraints compliance.
elements.sent-2411-164: Our approach can generalize and transfer knowledge across different type of circuits, including new, unseen ones.
elements.sent-2411-165: The generated floorplans not only outperform established baselines but also, when integrated into a procedural generation pipeline, yield complete layouts of comparable quality to human generated ones in significantly reduced runtime.
elements.sent-2411-166: In the future, we aim to augment the floorplan algorithm with detailed routing information to further condition device placement towards easier and more efficient routing configurations.. \bibliographystyle{IEEEtran.}
